extra.reasoning:
The monotonicity property is VIOLATED. The witness is the "after" state (stiff_neck=true) of the counterexample pair; the "before" state has stiff_neck=false with all other inputs identical.

Compare:
- BEFORE: syncope=true, stiff_neck=false, focal_weakness=false, rash=false, dyspnea=false. Matching rules: only r3 (syncope -> urgent, priority 2). No other rule fires (r1/r4/r5 need dyspnea/focal_weakness/rash; r2 needs stiff_neck). Result: acuity = urgent.
- AFTER: syncope=true, stiff_neck=true, focal_weakness=false, rash=false, dyspnea=false. Matching rules: r2 (syncope AND stiff_neck -> self_care, priority 100) and r3 (syncope -> urgent, priority 2). Highest priority wins: r2 at priority 100 dominates r3 at priority 2. Result: acuity = self_care.

Enum order low-to-high: self_care < routine < urgent < emergent. Setting stiff_neck from false to true lowered acuity from urgent to self_care, which is strictly lower. This violates the property that setting stiff_neck true must never lower acuity.

Root cause: rule r2 has the highest priority (100) yet assigns the LOWEST acuity (self_care), and its activation depends on stiff_neck becoming true. Whenever syncope is already true (so the baseline is urgent via r3, or emergent via r1/r4/r5 if those also hold), flipping stiff_neck to true activates r2, which overrides everything down to self_care. The chosen witness is the minimal one (only syncope and stiff_neck true).
